nodes.label: Data Pipeline
nodes.mechanism_description: Controls how raw text/code becomes token sequences presented to the model. Choices here shape distribution, context construction, and the effective learning signal under a fixed compute budget.

nodes.label: Neural Network
nodes.mechanism_description: Defines the parameterized function that maps tokens to next-token probabilities. Under Parameter Golf, architecture choices must trade off accuracy against strict parameter and memory ceilings.

nodes.label: Training & Evaluation
nodes.mechanism_description: Specifies how the model is optimized and scored, including loss design, optimizer state, precision, and benchmark harness constraints. These choices strongly affect peak memory and convergence at small scale.

nodes.label: Data Sources
nodes.mechanism_description: Determines the domains and genres the model observes during training. Source composition controls coverage, bias, and the kind of generalization the model can learn.

nodes.label: Web Text
nodes.mechanism_description: Broad natural-language text from the web provides diverse topics and writing styles. It tends to increase general knowledge but can introduce noise and duplication.

nodes.label: Code
nodes.mechanism_description: Programming language corpora emphasize syntax, long-range dependencies, and precise token patterns. Code data often improves reasoning-like behaviors at small model sizes.

nodes.label: Books / Long-Form
nodes.mechanism_description: Long-form text increases exposure to extended discourse structure and narrative coherence. It can help models learn longer-context patterns even with limited context windows.

nodes.label: Synthetic / Generated Data
nodes.mechanism_description: Synthetic data can target specific skills or failure modes by generating curated examples. It risks distribution shift or error amplification if generation quality is low.

nodes.label: Cleaning & Filtering
nodes.mechanism_description: Removes low-quality, duplicated, or harmful text before training. Better filtering usually increases effective data quality without increasing parameters.

nodes.label: Deduplication
nodes.mechanism_description: Detects and removes near-duplicate documents or passages to reduce memorization and wasted steps. This increases data efficiency at fixed compute.

nodes.label: Language ID Filter
nodes.mechanism_description: Keeps only target languages (or mixes them deliberately) to control distribution. It reduces accidental multilingual drift in a tiny model.

nodes.label: Quality Scoring Filter
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses heuristics or learned scoring to prioritize coherent, information-dense text. It effectively increases signal-to-noise ratio without changing architecture.

nodes.label: Safety / Toxicity Filter
nodes.mechanism_description: Removes or down-weights content associated with harmful or toxic language. It improves behavioral robustness with negligible model-side cost.

nodes.label: Tokenization
nodes.mechanism_description: Maps text into discrete tokens that define the model's input/output alphabet. Token choices affect sequence lengths, embedding size, and downstream memory/parameter budgets.

nodes.label: BPE (Subword)
nodes.mechanism_description: Byte Pair Encoding learns a subword vocabulary that balances token count and coverage. It is a standard choice for compact tokenization at scale.

nodes.label: Unigram (SentencePiece)
nodes.mechanism_description: Unigram tokenization chooses tokens via a probabilistic model, often yielding more flexible segmentations. It can reduce brittleness to rare substrings.

nodes.label: Byte-level BPE
nodes.mechanism_description: Operates on bytes before merging into subwords, ensuring full coverage of arbitrary text. It often increases sequence length slightly but avoids unknown tokens.

nodes.label: Character-level
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses characters as tokens, guaranteeing coverage but producing much longer sequences. This typically increases activation/KV-cache memory for a fixed context length.

nodes.label: Sequence Construction
nodes.mechanism_description: Defines how tokens are packed into fixed-length training sequences. Packing strategy changes utilization, padding waste, and effective batch size under memory limits.

nodes.label: Packing Strategy
nodes.mechanism_description: Controls how multiple documents are concatenated or separated to fill context windows. Better packing increases tokens-per-step at the same memory cost.

nodes.label: Fixed Concat + Padding
nodes.mechanism_description: Concatenates samples into fixed-size blocks with padding where needed. Simple to implement but can waste compute on padding tokens.

nodes.label: Dynamic Packing
nodes.mechanism_description: Dynamically packs variable-length samples to minimize padding and maximize real tokens per batch. This improves throughput under strict memory budgets.

nodes.label: Context Length
nodes.mechanism_description: Sets the maximum number of tokens processed per forward pass. Memory scales roughly linearly for activations and KV cache, making this a key Parameter Golf lever.

nodes.label: 256 Tokens
nodes.mechanism_description: Short context reduces activation and KV-cache memory, enabling larger batch sizes or wider models. It can hurt tasks requiring long-range dependencies.

nodes.label: 512 Tokens
nodes.mechanism_description: Moderate context is a common tradeoff between long-range modeling and memory cost. It keeps KV cache manageable while retaining some long-context capacity.

nodes.label: 1024 Tokens
nodes.mechanism_description: Long context increases both activation memory and KV cache substantially. This often forces reductions in model width/depth under a fixed memory ceiling.

nodes.label: Sampling & Batching
nodes.mechanism_description: Determines how examples are selected and grouped into batches. Batching decisions affect gradient noise, throughput, and peak memory during training.

nodes.label: Random Sampling
nodes.mechanism_description: Samples sequences uniformly or with simple weights, producing diverse batches. It is robust but can incur padding inefficiency with variable lengths.

nodes.label: Length-bucketed Batching
nodes.mechanism_description: Groups sequences of similar length to minimize padding and wasted compute. This increases effective tokens-per-step under a fixed memory budget.

nodes.label: Data Augmentation
nodes.mechanism_description: Applies transformations that change the training distribution without adding parameters. Augmentation can improve robustness but must preserve label semantics for next-token prediction.

nodes.label: Span Corruption / Masking
nodes.mechanism_description: Randomly masks contiguous spans and trains the model to predict missing tokens. This can encourage modeling longer dependencies and reduce overfitting.

nodes.label: Token Dropout
nodes.mechanism_description: Randomly drops or replaces tokens during training to discourage reliance on brittle cues. It is a lightweight way to increase robustness at tiny scale.

nodes.label: Contrastive Cropping
nodes.mechanism_description: Creates multiple cropped views of the same document to encourage consistent representations. It can help compact models learn invariances but adds pipeline complexity.

nodes.label: Curriculum & Mixing
nodes.mechanism_description: Controls how different data types or difficulties are scheduled over training. Curricula can improve convergence and sample-efficiency without increasing parameters.

nodes.label: Length Curriculum
nodes.mechanism_description: Starts with shorter sequences and gradually increases context length. This can stabilize early training and manage memory usage over time.

nodes.label: Dataset Mixing Weights
nodes.mechanism_description: Adjusts sampling probabilities across corpora to control the effective training distribution. Mixing is a powerful lever for tiny models where capacity is limited.

nodes.label: Embeddings
nodes.mechanism_description: Maps discrete tokens into continuous vectors the network can process. Embeddings often dominate parameter count in tiny LLMs because they scale with vocabulary size.

nodes.label: Token Embedding Strategy
nodes.mechanism_description: Defines how token IDs are converted to vectors and how much parameter budget is allocated to that mapping. Changing this is a common way to free parameters for the backbone.

nodes.label: Full Embedding Matrix
nodes.mechanism_description: A learned lookup table mapping each vocabulary token to a dense vector. It is expressive but can consume a large fraction of the parameter budget.

nodes.label: Factorized Embeddings
nodes.mechanism_description: Represents embeddings as a low-rank factorization to reduce parameters while preserving expressivity. This trades some embedding capacity for parameter headroom.

nodes.label: Hashed / Shared Embeddings
nodes.mechanism_description: Maps multiple tokens to shared embedding slots via hashing or sharing rules to reduce parameters. This can free significant budget but may introduce collisions that hurt accuracy.

nodes.label: Positional Encoding
nodes.mechanism_description: Injects token position information so the model can distinguish order. Some methods are parameter-free but affect attention behavior and memory scaling with context length.

nodes.label: RoPE (Rotary)
nodes.mechanism_description: Applies a deterministic rotation to query/key vectors based on position, enabling extrapolation to longer contexts. It is parameter-free and common in modern LLMs.

nodes.label: ALiBi (Linear Bias)
nodes.mechanism_description: Adds a head-dependent linear bias to attention logits that encodes relative positions. It is parameter-free and can improve long-context generalization under budget.

nodes.label: Learned Absolute Positions
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses a learned embedding for each position up to the context limit. It adds parameters proportional to context length and can be less robust to extrapolation.

nodes.label: Transformer Backbone
nodes.mechanism_description: Stacks attention and feed-forward blocks to model complex token dependencies. Under Parameter Golf, most innovation lives in reallocating parameter/memory across these sub-components.

nodes.label: Transformer Block
nodes.mechanism_description: A repeated unit combining attention, MLP, normalization, and residual connections. Its internal design determines both parameter efficiency and activation/KV-cache memory.

nodes.label: Attention
nodes.mechanism_description: Computes content-dependent mixing of token representations to model long-range interactions. Attention is memory-heavy due to attention logits/activations and KV cache during inference.

nodes.label: Attention Projections
nodes.mechanism_description: Linear layers that produce queries, keys, values, and output projections. Projection parameter count scales roughly with hidden size squared, making it a major budget lever.

nodes.label: Query Projection
nodes.mechanism_description: Projects hidden states into query vectors used to retrieve information from keys/values. Its parameters scale with hidden size and number of layers.

nodes.label: Key Projection
nodes.mechanism_description: Projects hidden states into key vectors that index information in attention. Key projection can be per-head (more parameters) or shared across heads (fewer parameters).

nodes.label: Per-head Keys (MHA-style)
nodes.mechanism_description: Computes distinct key vectors for each attention head, maximizing expressivity. This typically uses more parameters than shared-key variants.

nodes.label: Shared Keys (GQA/MQA-style)
nodes.mechanism_description: Shares key projections across heads or head groups, reducing parameter count. It often trades some expressivity for large memory savings in KV cache during inference.

nodes.label: Value Projection
nodes.mechanism_description: Projects hidden states into value vectors that carry content aggregated by attention weights. Like keys, values can be per-head or shared to reduce parameters and KV cache.

nodes.label: Per-head Values (MHA-style)
nodes.mechanism_description: Computes distinct value vectors per head to maximize representational capacity. This uses more parameters and increases KV cache size compared to shared-value variants.

nodes.label: Shared Values (GQA/MQA-style)
nodes.mechanism_description: Shares value projections across heads or head groups, reducing parameter count. It can significantly reduce inference KV cache memory at small parameter budgets.

nodes.label: Output Projection
nodes.mechanism_description: Projects concatenated head outputs back to the model hidden size. It is required for standard multi-head attention and scales with hidden size squared.

nodes.label: Attention Computation
nodes.mechanism_description: Computes attention weights and applies them to values. The choice of implementation affects peak activation memory and speed, especially at longer context lengths.

nodes.label: Softmax Attention Implementation
nodes.mechanism_description: Determines how attention logits and softmax are computed and stored. Memory-efficient implementations can reduce peak activation memory without changing parameters.

nodes.label: Naive / Standard Softmax
nodes.mechanism_description: Materializes attention logits/weights more directly, increasing activation memory at long context. It is widely supported but often memory-limited in Parameter Golf settings.

nodes.label: FlashAttention-style (Tiled)
nodes.mechanism_description: Computes attention in tiles to avoid storing full attention matrices. This can sharply reduce peak activation memory while preserving standard softmax attention.

nodes.label: KV Cache Strategy (Inference)
nodes.mechanism_description: Stores past keys/values for autoregressive decoding to avoid recomputation. KV cache memory scales with context length and head dimensions, often dominating inference memory.

nodes.label: No KV Cache (Recompute)
nodes.mechanism_description: Does not store past keys/values, recomputing them each step. This reduces memory but increases compute and can be infeasible for long contexts.

nodes.label: FP16/BF16 KV Cache
nodes.mechanism_description: Stores keys/values in half precision, balancing fidelity and memory usage. This is a common baseline for inference memory accounting.

nodes.label: INT8 KV Cache
nodes.mechanism_description: Quantizes KV cache to 8-bit representations to reduce memory footprint. This can introduce approximation error but may be critical under strict memory budgets.

nodes.label: Feed-Forward Network (MLP)
nodes.mechanism_description: Applies per-token nonlinear transformations that expand and contract hidden representations. MLP layers often dominate parameter count and a large share of activation memory.

nodes.label: MLP Variant
nodes.mechanism_description: Chooses the internal structure of the MLP (activation, gating, sparsity, or factorization). This is a prime target for parameter reallocation toward OOD ideas.

nodes.label: Dense FFN (ReLU/GELU)
nodes.mechanism_description: Standard two-layer feed-forward network with a pointwise nonlinearity. It is a strong baseline but uses many parameters for a given hidden size.

nodes.label: SwiGLU (Gated MLP)
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses a gated activation that can improve quality at fixed depth. It often increases parameters slightly versus a dense FFN but can be more compute-efficient per gain.

nodes.label: Low-rank / Factorized MLP
nodes.mechanism_description: Factorizes large MLP matrices into low-rank components to reduce parameters. This can free budget for attention or new modules but may bottleneck capacity.

nodes.label: Mixture-of-Experts (Sparse)
nodes.mechanism_description: Routes tokens to a subset of expert MLPs, increasing total parameters without proportional compute. It can boost capacity but complicates routing and memory accounting.

nodes.label: Normalization
nodes.mechanism_description: Stabilizes activations and gradients to enable deep training. Normalization has low parameter cost but strongly affects optimization dynamics in small models.

nodes.label: LayerNorm
nodes.mechanism_description: Normalizes activations using mean and variance across hidden dimensions with learned gain/bias. It is widely used but can be slower than RMSNorm in some kernels.

nodes.label: RMSNorm
nodes.mechanism_description: Normalizes by root-mean-square without subtracting the mean, typically using only a learned gain. It can be more parameter- and compute-efficient while remaining stable.

nodes.label: Residual Connections
nodes.mechanism_description: Adds skip connections that improve gradient flow and allow iterative refinement of representations. Residuals add negligible parameters but contribute to activation storage.

nodes.label: Dropout
nodes.mechanism_description: Randomly zeroes activations during training to reduce overfitting. It has no parameters but can change optimization behavior and effective capacity in tiny models.

nodes.label: Output Head
nodes.mechanism_description: Maps final hidden states to logits over the vocabulary. The output layer can match embedding size and may rival embeddings in parameter cost unless weights are tied or factorized.

nodes.label: LM Head Strategy
nodes.mechanism_description: Defines how logits are produced, including whether the head is untied, tied to embeddings, or structured to reduce parameters. This is a common place to free parameters under strict budgets.

nodes.label: Untied Linear LM Head
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses a separate weight matrix to map hidden states to vocabulary logits. This maximizes flexibility but can nearly double parameters when embeddings are also large.

nodes.label: Weight-tied Head (Reuse Embeddings)
nodes.mechanism_description: Reuses the token embedding matrix as the output projection, reducing parameters and sometimes improving generalization. This is a high-leverage Parameter Golf trick.

nodes.label: Adaptive / Factorized Softmax
nodes.mechanism_description: Structures the output distribution to reduce compute and parameters for rare tokens. It can save parameters but adds complexity and may affect benchmark comparability.

nodes.label: Loss Function
nodes.mechanism_description: Defines the training objective that shapes gradients. Loss modifications can change calibration and stability without adding parameters to the model.

nodes.label: Cross-Entropy
nodes.mechanism_description: Maximizes the likelihood of the next token under the model distribution. It is the standard objective for language modeling and typically parameter-free.

nodes.label: Label Smoothing
nodes.mechanism_description: Blends the one-hot target with a uniform distribution to reduce overconfidence. This can improve generalization and calibration in small models.

nodes.label: Z-loss / Logit Penalty
nodes.mechanism_description: Adds a penalty to stabilize logits and improve numerical behavior, often helpful with low precision. It can improve training stability without changing model size.

nodes.label: Optimizer & State
nodes.mechanism_description: Controls parameter updates and determines additional optimizer-state memory. Optimizer choice is a major contributor to peak training memory at fixed parameter counts.

nodes.label: Optimizer State Strategy
nodes.mechanism_description: Represents the extra state stored per parameter (e.g., moments) and its precision. Under tight memory budgets, quantized or factorized states can free memory for larger batches or models.

nodes.label: AdamW (FP32 moments)
nodes.mechanism_description: Stores first and second moment estimates for each parameter, typically in FP32 for stability. This is effective but can dominate training memory for tiny models.

nodes.label: AdamW (8-bit moments)
nodes.mechanism_description: Quantizes optimizer moments to reduce memory while approximating Adam updates. This can significantly lower peak memory with modest accuracy impact when tuned.

nodes.label: Adafactor (Factorized)
nodes.mechanism_description: Approximates second-moment statistics with factored representations to reduce memory. It is often used when optimizer-state memory is the main bottleneck.

nodes.label: SGD + Momentum
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses momentum buffers but avoids second-moment estimates, reducing state memory. It can be harder to tune for transformers but is memory-light.

nodes.label: Numerical Precision
nodes.mechanism_description: Chooses numeric formats for weights, activations, and gradients. Lower precision reduces memory and bandwidth but can require stabilization tricks to train reliably.

nodes.label: FP32 Training
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses 32-bit floats for most tensors, offering stability at high memory cost. This often violates Parameter Golf memory constraints for longer contexts.

nodes.label: FP16/BF16 Mixed Precision
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses half precision for activations/weights with selective FP32 accumulations. It is a standard approach to fit larger models/batches under memory limits.

nodes.label: FP8 Activations (Advanced)
nodes.mechanism_description: Uses 8-bit floats for some tensors to further reduce memory and bandwidth. It can provide large savings but often needs careful scaling and kernel support.

nodes.label: Activation Storage Strategy
nodes.mechanism_description: Controls how intermediate activations are stored for backpropagation. Checkpointing can reduce peak memory by recomputing activations at the cost of extra compute.

nodes.label: Store All Activations
nodes.mechanism_description: Keeps forward activations for backpropagation, minimizing recomputation. This is compute-efficient but often hits memory ceilings first.

nodes.label: Gradient Checkpointing
nodes.mechanism_description: Recomputes some forward passes during backpropagation instead of storing all activations. This reduces peak memory and can unlock larger batch sizes in Parameter Golf.

nodes.label: Evaluation Harness
nodes.mechanism_description: Defines how the model is scored and constrained, including exact benchmark implementation. A stable harness is required to compare innovations under the same Parameter Golf rules.

nodes.label: OpenAI Parameter Golf Benchmark
nodes.mechanism_description: Scores models under strict parameter and memory constraints, preventing trivial scaling. The benchmark incentivizes architectural and optimization innovations that fit tight budgets.

nodes.label: Budget Accounting
nodes.mechanism_description: Tracks parameter count and memory usage to enforce constraints and guide component swaps. Accurate accounting enables deliberate tradeoffs rather than accidental OOM failures.

nodes.label: Parameter Counter
nodes.mechanism_description: Computes model parameter count, optionally by component, to ensure compliance with size limits. It enables targeted parameter reallocation toward novel modules.

nodes.label: Peak Memory Estimator
nodes.mechanism_description: Estimates peak memory from activations, optimizer state, and KV cache to prevent exceeding constraints. It supports 'swap out' reasoning for memory-heavy components like attention.
